content.text: Tags are key-value pairs you attach to AWS resources. Every AWS resource that supports tagging accepts up to 50 tags, each with a key up to 128 characters and a value up to 256 characters. Tags are free to add and do not affect resource behavior — they are purely metadata labels that you and AWS services can read.
A tag has two parts: a
(the specific attribute). For example, the key
marks a resource as part of the production environment. The key
assigns it to a billing cost center. Multiple tags together describe a resource from multiple dimensions simultaneously.

Tags solve a fundamental problem: AWS accounts accumulate hundreds or thousands of resources across dozens of services. Without tags, answering questions like "how much does our payment service cost?" or "which resources belong to the data engineering team?" requires manual cross-referencing. With consistent tagging, these questions become simple queries.
Tags and Resource Groups — How They Relate

attrs.alt: Diagram showing tags on individual resources being queried to form a Resource Group, with use cases for operations, cost, compliance, and automation

content.text: is a named collection of AWS resources defined by a tag-based query. The query runs continuously — when you add a new resource and tag it to match the query, it automatically joins the group. When you remove matching tags, it leaves. Resource Groups are dynamic: you define the rules once, and membership updates automatically.

The relationship is: tags describe individual resources; Resource Groups aggregate tagged resources into logical units. This two-layer model gives you fine-grained control (per-resource tags) and broad operational views (group-level actions) simultaneously.
A tagging strategy that works at scale requires agreement before you start. Adding tags retroactively to thousands of resources is painful — define your schema upfront.
Most organizations need five to eight core tags that apply to every resource:
Separate environments; control access with IAM
Identify which app owns the resource
Track how the resource was provisioned
Keep keys consistent in casing and format. Choose one convention (
are different keys — mixing them breaks tag-based queries and cost reports.
key prefix for its own system tags (e.g.,
prefix for your own tags — it is prohibited and the API will reject them.
Use title case for keys that team members will type: Environment, Application, Team
Use lowercase for values to avoid case sensitivity issues: prod (not Prod or PROD)
Avoid spaces in keys and values — use hyphens or underscores. Spaces work in the API but cause problems in CLI filters and automation scripts
Document your tag schema in a runbook or wiki — without documentation, teams invent their own key names
Using free-form values without a controlled vocabulary. If some engineers tag
, cost reports split the data and group-based queries miss half the resources. Define and enforce a finite set of allowed values.

Every AWS resource creation wizard includes a Tags step. Applying tags at creation time is the most reliable approach — the resource is tagged from its first billing hour and appears in group queries immediately.
In CloudFormation and CDK, tags are applied in the resource definition. Tags defined at the stack level propagate automatically to all supported resources in the stack:

# CloudFormation stack-level tags (propagate to all resources)
Tags:
  - Key: Environment
    Value: prod
  - Key: Application
    Value: payment-service
  - Key: ManagedBy
    Value: cloudformation

Resources:
  WebServer:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Instance
    Properties:
      ImageId: ami-0abcdef1234567890
      InstanceType: t3.medium
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: web-server-01
        # Environment, Application, ManagedBy inherited from stack
To tag existing resources in bulk, use the
— a console tool found by searching "Tag Editor" in the global search bar. Tag Editor lets you:
Choose one or more regions to search across.
Select resource types (EC2 instances, S3 buckets, Lambda functions, etc.) or choose "All supported resource types".
Add optional tag filters to narrow results (e.g., find resources that have no Environment tag).
Run the search — results show a table of matching resources with their current tags.
Select resources in the table and click "Manage tags" to add, edit, or remove tags in bulk.

Tag Editor workflow:
  AWS Console → search "Tag Editor" → Find resources
  
  Region(s):    us-east-1, us-west-2
  Resource type: All supported resource types
  Tag filters:   Environment (key exists: No)  ← find untagged resources
  
  → Search
  → Results: 47 untagged resources found
  → Select all → Manage tags
  → Add tag: Environment = prod
  → Apply tags → Confirm

If your account is part of an AWS Organization, administrators can enforce tag policies using the Organizations Tag Policies feature. A tag policy defines required keys, allowed values, and key capitalization rules. Resources that violate the policy are flagged in compliance reports, and (optionally) tag actions that violate the policy are blocked.
key on EC2 instances, RDS databases, Lambda functions, and S3 buckets, and limits values to the four approved choices. Resources with non-compliant tags appear in the Tag Policy compliance dashboard.
Resource Groups are created and managed in the
service (search for it in the console). There are two types:

The most common type. You define a tag query, and the group dynamically includes all resources matching that query. The group updates automatically as resources are created, tagged, untagged, or deleted.

Create Resource Group:
  Name: prod-web-frontend
  Group type: Tag based

  Tag-based criteria:
    Key: Environment    Value: prod    (required)
    Key: Application    Value: web-frontend    (required)

  Resource types: All supported resource types
  (or limit to: AWS::EC2::Instance, AWS::RDS::DBInstance, AWS::Lambda::Function)

  → Preview group resources (shows matching resources in real time)
  → Create group

If you use CloudFormation, you can create a Resource Group that contains all resources in a specific CloudFormation stack. No manual tag-based query needed — the stack membership is the query. This is useful for grouping resources that were deployed together even if you haven't applied custom tags.
Stack-based groups reflect current stack state: resources added to the stack join the group; resources removed from the stack leave it.
Once a Resource Group exists, other AWS services can target it directly, enabling group-level operations without listing individual resource ARNs.
AWS Systems Manager (SSM) integrates with Resource Groups for bulk operations:

# Run a command on all instances in a resource group (via SSM Run Command)
aws ssm send-command   --targets "Key=resource-groups:Name,Values=prod-web-frontend"   --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript"   --parameters '{"commands":["sudo systemctl restart nginx"]}'   --region us-east-1

# Check patch compliance for the group
aws ssm describe-instance-patch-states-for-patch-group   --patch-group prod-web-frontend   --region us-east-1
This approach scales safely — you do not need to enumerate instance IDs. New instances that match the group's tag query are automatically included in future Run Command operations.
CloudWatch lets you create dashboards that use tag-based resource queries. When you add a metric widget, you can scope it to resources matching a tag:

CloudWatch → Dashboards → Create dashboard
  → Add widget → Metrics
  → Browse → EC2 → Per-Instance Metrics
  → Filter by tag: Environment=prod, Application=web-frontend
  → Add CPUUtilization, NetworkIn, StatusCheckFailed
  → The dashboard automatically includes new instances with matching tags
AWS Cost Explorer and the Cost and Usage Report support filtering by tags — but only tags that you explicitly activate as
. Without activation, tag data does not appear in billing reports.
Go to the Billing console (search "Billing" in the console).
In the left navigation, click "Cost allocation tags".
You see two sections: AWS-generated tags (e.g., aws:createdBy) and user-defined tags.
Select the tag keys you want to appear in cost reports (e.g., Environment, Application, CostCenter, Team).
Click "Activate". Activation takes up to 24 hours to take effect.
After activation, new billing data includes the tag values. Historical data is not retroactively tagged.
Once activated, Cost Explorer lets you group and filter costs by these tags:

Cost Explorer:
  Group by: Tag → Environment
  → Shows cost breakdown:
       prod:     $8,420/month
       staging:  $1,230/month
       dev:      $340/month

  Filter by: Tag → Application = payment-service
  → Shows total cost of the payment service across all resources and regions

The Cost and Usage Report (CUR) is a detailed CSV that includes one column per activated cost allocation tag. This enables fully automated cost attribution in financial systems, data warehouses, or business intelligence tools:

Sample CUR columns (simplified):
  lineItem/ProductCode, lineItem/UsageType, lineItem/UnblendedCost,
  resourceTags/user:Environment, resourceTags/user:Application, resourceTags/user:Team
  
  Example row:
  AmazonEC2, BoxUsage:t3.medium, 0.0416,
  prod, web-frontend, platform

Tags enable dynamic access control through IAM policy conditions. Instead of writing policies that enumerate specific resource ARNs, you write policies that grant or deny access based on tags — the policy applies automatically to any resource with matching tags.
This policy allows starting, stopping, and rebooting EC2 instances only when the instance's
. The policy scales to any number of teams without updating the policy when teams change.
You can use IAM to require that engineers apply specific tags when creating resources:
This policy denies EC2 instance launches that don't include both the
tags. Engineers who forget to tag their instances receive an explicit access denied error, which is a faster feedback loop than discovering untagged resources in a compliance scan days later.

Beyond the console Tag Editor, AWS Resource Explorer provides a cross-region, cross-service resource search that integrates with the global console search bar. Resource Explorer maintains an index of all resources in your account, updated in near-real-time.

# Search for all Lambda functions tagged prod
aws resource-explorer-2 search   --query-string "resourcetype:AWS::Lambda::Function tag:Environment=prod"   --region us-east-1

# Search for untagged EC2 instances (missing required tag)
aws resource-explorer-2 search   --query-string "resourcetype:AWS::EC2::Instance -tag:Environment"   --region us-east-1

# Find all resources tagged with a specific application
aws resource-explorer-2 search   --query-string "tag:Application=payment-service"   --region us-east-1   --output json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for r in data['Resources']:
    print(r['ResourceType'], r['Arn'])

When resources are managed by infrastructure as code (IaC) tools, tags are defined alongside the resource configuration rather than applied manually. This ensures every resource is tagged correctly from the moment it is created, with no human step that can be skipped.
In Terraform, tags can be set on individual resources or applied globally via a
In CDK, tags applied to a stack are inherited by all resources in that stack:

import aws_cdk as cdk
from aws_cdk import Tags

app = cdk.App()
stack = WebStack(app, "WebStack",
    env=cdk.Environment(account="123456789012", region="us-east-1")
)

# Stack-level tags propagate to all resources
Tags.of(stack).add("Environment", "prod")
Tags.of(stack).add("Team", "platform")
Tags.of(stack).add("ManagedBy", "cdk")
Tags.of(stack).add("CostCenter", "cc-1001")
Auditing Tag Compliance with AWS Config
AWS Config can continuously evaluate whether your resources comply with your tagging requirements. A Config rule checks resources against a policy and marks them as COMPLIANT or NON_COMPLIANT.
managed Config rule checks that specified tags exist on resources. Configure which resource types to check and which keys are required:

NON_COMPLIANT resources appear in the Config console dashboard and can trigger automated remediation via SSM Automation or EventBridge rules — for example, notifying the owning team via SNS when a new untagged resource is detected.
Understanding what goes wrong with tagging in practice helps you avoid the same issues:
Inconsistent casing — Environment vs environment vs ENVIRONMENT creates three separate cost buckets and breaks tag-based queries. A tag policy enforces one convention.

Missing tags on auto-scaled instances — EC2 Auto Scaling groups can propagate tags to launched instances, but you must configure this in the launch template's tag specifications. Default behavior is no propagation.
Not tagging EBS volumes — EC2 instance tags do not automatically apply to attached EBS volumes. Tag both the instance and its volumes, or configure the launch template to tag volumes at creation.
Forgetting Lambda and Step Functions — these are easy to overlook in audits. Tag Editor's 'All supported resource types' query finds them alongside EC2 and RDS.
Activating cost allocation tags too late — historical billing data is not retroactively tagged. Activate cost allocation tags when you first set up an account, not after you need cost breakdowns.
Tag drift over time — teams rename applications or reorganize cost centers without updating tags on existing resources. Schedule quarterly tag audits using Tag Editor or Config compliance reports.

Tags are key-value labels attached to AWS resources that enable cost attribution, access control, compliance auditing, and operational automation. A consistent tagging strategy with defined key names, controlled value vocabularies, and enforcement via Tag Policies is foundational to managing AWS at scale. Resource Groups aggregate tagged resources into dynamic logical collections that other AWS services (Systems Manager, CloudWatch, Cost Explorer) can target directly. Cost allocation tags must be explicitly activated in the Billing console before tag data appears in cost reports. Tag-based IAM conditions allow dynamic, scalable access control that adapts automatically when new resources are created with matching tags.
